title: All-optical saddle trap as a platform for mesoscopic quantum experiments
authors: Pedro V. Paraguassú, Luca Abrahão, Daniel Tandeitnik, D. Martínez-Tibaduiza, Antonio Zelaquett Khoury, Thiago Guerreiro
abstract: We investigate the quantum dynamics of a levitated nanoparticle in a structured light rotating saddle-like optical potential consisting of a superposition of Gaussian and Laguerre-Gauss modes with detuned frequencies. This rotating saddle trap offers unique opportunities for quantum experiments, such as reduced decoherence due to photon recoil and absorption, the possibility of large delocalization of the particle's center-of-mass motion, particle recovery protocols, the generation of motional entanglement and momentum squeezing. As an application, we show that this saddle-trap architecture enables force detection with sensitivity in the zepto-Newton regime.
summary: The paper proposes a new all-optical saddle trap for levitated nanoparticles using structured light to reduce decoherence. This platform allows for advanced quantum tasks such as controlled wavefunction expansion and highly sensitive force detection in the zepto-Newton range.
main_problem: The need for levitated optomechanical platforms that minimize decoherence from photon recoil and thermal radiation while enabling controlled quantum experiments like delocalization and force sensing.
main_result: An all-optical rotating saddle trap using structured light (Gaussian and Laguerre-Gauss modes) suppresses photon recoil decoherence and enables force detection sensitivity in the zepto-Newton regime.
method: The study uses a master equation approach to model decoherence and a Quantum Fisher Information Matrix analysis to derive fundamental limits for force metrology.
model_or_system: A mesoscopic, levitated nanoparticle (radius 50 nm) in a rotating, time-dependent quadratic potential created by a superposition of Gaussian and Laguerre-Gauss optical modes.
key_observables: State purity, logarithmic negativity (entanglement), momentum squeezing, and minimum detectable force.
important_parameters: LG mode intensity fraction (IL), rotation frequency (Omega), gas-limited coherence time (Tgas), and numerical aperture (NA).
main_assumptions: The system is treated within the dipole limit and quadratic approximation, assuming the particle motion remains within a characteristic length scale L.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows purity evolution; Figure 2 illustrates particle delocalization and recovery protocols; Figure 3 plots entanglement vs. rotation frequency; Supplemental Figure S1 details trap stability and depth.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the saddle trap architecture, analyzes the stability and validity of the quadratic approximation, quantifies decoherence suppression and entanglement generation, and concludes with a metrological application for force detection.
why_interesting: It provides a novel platform for studying open quantum systems by demonstrating how structured light can be engineered to suppress specific decoherence channels like photon recoil.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Time evolution of the state purity, P for dif- ferent fractional powers IL of the l = ±2 modes in the saddle trap, for an initial thermal state of a Gaussian trap with occupation numbers ¯nx = ¯ny = 0.8 and initial purity P(0) ≈0.6.

FIG. 2: Time evolution of (a) the position uncertainty normalized by the particle radius, ∆x/R. At t = 0, the particle is transferred into a slowly rotating saddle beam (Ω/Ω0 < 1), leading to state expansion. At t = 0.2 ms, the rotation frequency is abruptly increased to Ω/Ω0 = 4.5, truncating state expansion. (b) Time evolution of the conditional mean position ⟨x⟩/R under continuous monitoring and feedback cooling, starting from an initial displacement ⟨x⟩0 = R.

FIG. 3: Maximum entanglement at 1 ms, quantified by the logarithmic negativity LN , as a function of the Laguerre-Gaussian fractional power IL and the dimen- sionless rotation frequency Ω/Ω0. The lower and upper horizontal axes display the degree of rotation and the La- guerre mode fraction IL, respectively.

FIG. 4: Time evolution of (a) the momentum uncer- tainty normalized by zero-point fluctuations of the initial Gaussian trap as discussed in the main text, ∆px/pzpf, specifically for IL = 90%. The dashed line marks the zero-point fluctuation limit, where values below it in- dicate momentum squeezing. (b) Minimum detectable force Fx,min as a function of time for different Laguerre mode fractions IL. The sensitivity is derived from the quantum Cramér-Rao Bound with a stable rotation fre- quency Ω= 20 Ω0. Increasing IL enhances momentum squeezing, directly improving sensitivity and pushing the detection limit into the zepto-Newton (10−21 N) regime.
